title: Listening - Part 4: Working in Sports Physiotherapy
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Nina, a sports physiotherapist. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today I’m joined by Nina Shah, a physiotherapist who works with runners and footballers. Nina, what made you choose this profession? || Nina: At sixteen I was playing basketball and injured my knee. I expected treatment to consist of someone telling me to rest, but the physiotherapist gave me exercises and explained every stage of recovery. [24]The experience showed me that helping someone return confidently to an activity could be just as satisfying as competing[/24]. That changed my ideas about a career. || Presenter: Did your training match your expectations? || Nina: I imagined learning lots of clever treatments, and we did study the body in detail. But patients do not recover according to a perfect schedule. [25]My placements taught me that encouraging someone through slow progress requires as much patience as scientific knowledge[/25]. A good plan is useless if a patient loses confidence and stops following it. || Presenter: You now treat both professionals and people who exercise for enjoyment. Are they very different? || Nina: The professional athlete may have a team and a deadline, while an amateur may be fitting treatment around work. However, [26]for both, being unable to do the sport they love can affect their mood as much as their physical condition[/26]. I try not to assume that a famous player's goal is more important than anyone else's. || Presenter: There seems to be a lot of technology in sport these days. || Nina: It can be helpful. Sensors can show how evenly somebody lands after a jump, which is difficult to judge by eye. But [27]a screen cannot tell you that a patient is frightened of reinjury unless you ask and listen[/27]. I use measurements to support a conversation, never to replace it. || Presenter: What is the hardest professional decision you have to make? || Nina: Often it is telling an enthusiastic athlete they are not ready for competition. They may feel perfectly well during ordinary movement. [28]Saying no is difficult when someone is desperate to play, but returning too soon could turn a minor injury into a long-term one[/28]. My role is to protect their future, not simply please them today. || Presenter: You work with coaches too. What has that taught you? || Nina: Initially I worried coaches would only want fast results. Most understand that recovery takes time. [29]The best outcomes happen when the coach, athlete and physiotherapist all agree on what the person can safely do in training[/29]. Then there is no confusion and the athlete still feels part of the team. || Presenter: What advice would you offer future physiotherapists? || Nina: Study hard, of course, because there is a lot to understand. But do not assume the person on the treatment table is only a damaged ankle or shoulder. [30]Ask what they are worried about and what returning to activity means to them[/30]. Recovery is more successful when people feel you understand their lives as well as their injury.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: Why did Nina decide to become a physiotherapist?

options.A: She wanted to continue competitive basketball.
options.B: Her own treatment showed her the value of supporting recovery.
options.C: A coach suggested it after she was injured.

questions.explanation: Nina's injury made her realise how satisfying helping someone return to activity could be.

questions.question: What did Nina learn during her placements?

options.A: Patience is an important part of treatment.
options.B: Most patients expect to recover quickly.
options.C: Scientific knowledge matters less than confidence.

questions.explanation: She learned that slow progress demands patience as well as scientific knowledge.

questions.question: What does Nina say about professional and amateur athletes?

options.A: Amateurs are less prepared to follow treatment plans.
options.C: Injury can affect both groups emotionally.

questions.explanation: She says inability to do a loved sport can affect the mood of either group.

questions.question: What is Nina's view of technology?

options.A: It is useful but cannot replace speaking to patients.
options.B: It can assess fear of reinjury more accurately.
options.C: It is mainly suitable for professional athletes.

questions.explanation: She uses measurement to support conversations, because a screen cannot reveal a patient's fears.

questions.question: What does Nina sometimes find difficult?

options.B: Stopping an athlete returning too early.

questions.explanation: She finds it hard to say no when athletes want to play but might worsen an injury.

questions.question: According to Nina, when is recovery most effective?

options.A: When the athlete trains separately from the team.
options.C: When everyone shares a safe training plan.

questions.explanation: She values agreement between coach, athlete and physiotherapist.

questions.question: What advice does Nina give future physiotherapists?

options.A: Understand the person's concerns and aims.
options.B: Choose one type of sport to specialise in.

questions.explanation: Nina advises asking about worries and what returning to activity means to the patient.
